did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper used machine learning classifiers, specifically K-Nearest Neighbors (KNN), Artificial Neural Networks (ANN), and Support Vector Machines (SVM), to classify skin lesions into benign, malignant (melanoma), and seborrheic keratosis categories. The best performing model was a 2nd order polynomial SVM, achieving 95.8% overall accuracy on a dataset of 2481 dermoscopy images.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work involving the development and validation of an automated classification system for skin lesions using machine learning classifiers (KNN, ANN, SVM) on a dataset of 2481 dermoscopy images. It includes specific sections for Methods (data acquisition, preprocessing, segmentation, feature extraction, classification) and Results (accuracy, sensitivity, precision metrics), which are characteristic of a primary research study rather than a review.

title: Automated Classification of Skin Lesions Using Different Classifiers
journal: 6th International Conference on Engineering Technology and its Applications
brief_summary: This study developed an automated classification system for skin lesions to distinguish between benign, malignant melanoma, and seborrheic keratosis categories. The researchers utilized a dataset of 2481 dermoscopy images, applying preprocessing steps including hair removal and feature extraction from RGB, YCbCr, and HSV color spaces. Three machine learning classifiers—K-Nearest Neighbors (KNN), Artificial Neural Networks (ANN), and Support Vector Machines (SVM)—were evaluated on both segmented and non-segmented image groups. The best-performing model was a 2nd order polynomial SVM applied to non-segmented images, which achieved an overall accuracy of 95.8%. The model demonstrated high sensitivity and precision for all classes, with the non-segmented approach outperforming the segmented one by leveraging surrounding tissue information.

lesion_types_studied.details: One of the three main classes in the dataset, consisting of 1635 images. The paper notes that 'benign melanoma' is a term used in the text for non-cancerous growths, though clinically this likely refers to benign nevi or other benign lesions.
One of the three main classes in the dataset, consisting of 463 images. Described as growing quickly and spreading easily.
One of the three main classes in the dataset, consisting of 383 images. Described as the most common type of benign melanoma skin growth, usually brown, black, or light tan, and not harmful.

all_task_types.target: Discrete diagnostic categories of skin lesions (Benign, Malignant melanoma, Seborrheic keratosis)
all_task_types.details: The paper performs multiclass classification of skin lesions into three discrete classes: benign, malignant melanoma, and seborrheic keratosis. The study evaluates K-Nearest Neighbors (KNN), Artificial Neural Networks (ANN), and Support Vector Machines (SVM) classifiers. The best performing model was a 2nd order polynomial SVM applied to non-segmented images, achieving 95.8% overall accuracy, 97% sensitivity for benign, 91.4% for melanoma, and 99.7% for seborrheic keratosis.

datasets_used.name: Custom Dermoscopy Dataset
datasets_used.description: A dataset of 2481 dermoscopy images acquired for the study, imported using MATLAB R2021b. The paper does not specify if this is a public benchmark, private collection, or from a specific hospital, but describes it as a dataset used for developing and validating the classification system.

use.training: 70% of the data (1737 images) was used to train the KNN, ANN, and SVM classifiers.
use.testing: 30% of the data (744 images) was used to test the network and estimate model prediction/discrimination.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance was evaluated using accuracy, sensitivity, and precision metrics.

Custom Dermoscopy Dataset (Non-segmented.notes: The 2nd order polynomial SVM on non-segmented images achieved the best overall accuracy of 95.8%. The overall accuracy in the training stage was 99.9%, and in the test stage was 86.4%.
Sensitivity values for the best model (2nd order polynomial SVM) on non-segmented data.
Precision values for the best model (2nd order polynomial SVM) on non-segmented data.

Specific counts from the confusion matrix analysis. For melanoma, accuracy was 90.7% with 77.3% precision in the tested confusion matrix context mentioned in the text, though Table 3 lists 96.6% precision for the overall non-segmented result.

Custom Dermoscopy Dataset (Segmented ima.notes: The paper states that accuracy is better in the non-segmented case compared to the segmented case, attributing this to the role of surrounding tissues in distinguishing between classes.

architecture_used.main_model: Support Vector Machine (SVM) with 2nd order polynomial kernel (best performing), K-Nearest Neighbors (KNN), and Artificial Neural Network (ANN)

SVM.notes: Best performing model on non-segmented images

ANN.details: Used as one of the evaluated classifiers; specific architecture details (layers, neurons) not reported in context

KNN.details: Used as one of the evaluated classifiers; specific k-value not reported in context

preprocessing_techniques: Hair removal using a morphological approach: converting RGB to grayscale, applying a flat structuring element, applying a bottom-hat filter, and using a median filter for hair removal, applied separately to each RGB channel before regenerating the RGB image.

Image segmentation via thresholding: converting images to HSV color space, applying Otsu thresholding to the Hue channel, creating a mask of the largest area, dilating the mask to fill holes, and multiplying the mask with the original RGB image to obtain segmented lesions.
Feature extraction from multiple color spaces: extracting mean, standard deviation, entropy, and skewness features from RGB, YCbCr, and HSV color spaces.
Texture feature extraction: applying Gabor filters to extract multiscale and multi-directional characteristics, followed by extracting mean and standard deviation from the Gabor filter outputs.
conclusions: The study successfully developed an automated classification method for distinguishing between three main classes of skin lesions: benign, malignant melanoma, and seborrheic keratosis.
Among the tested classifiers (KNN, ANN, and SVM), the 2nd order polynomial Support Vector Machine (SVM) achieved the best performance.
The best-performing model (2nd order polynomial SVM) achieved an overall accuracy of 95.8% on non-segmented images.
The non-segmented approach outperformed the segmented approach, with the authors attributing this to the informative role of surrounding tissues in distinguishing between classes.

The model demonstrated high sensitivity and precision for all classes: 97.0% sensitivity/92.4% precision for benign, 91.4% sensitivity/96.6% precision for melanoma, and 99.7% sensitivity/99.7% precision for seborrheic keratosis.
Automatically extracted features (without reduction via PCA or selection) were found to be efficient and valid for the estimation of the final model.
The authors suggest the method could be extended for everyday clinical demand, potentially via a mobile phone application using standard cameras to reduce the burden on medical professions.
important_remarks: The study uses a custom, non-public dataset of 2481 images (1635 benign, 463 malignant melanoma, 383 seborrheic keratosis) with no mention of external validation or standard benchmark datasets (e.g., ISIC), limiting generalizability.
The paper does not report formal statistical significance testing (p-values, confidence intervals) for the performance differences between classifiers or segmentation methods.

There is a notable discrepancy in reported metrics: while the abstract and conclusion state 95.8% overall accuracy, the results section reports 86.4% accuracy on the test set and 99.9% on the training set, suggesting potential overfitting or inconsistent reporting of train/test performance.
The dataset is imbalanced, with benign lesions comprising 65.9% of the data, which may bias the classifier towards the majority class.

The authors explicitly state that feature selection was not performed and all 71 automatically extracted features were used, which they claim are efficient, though high-dimensional feature spaces without selection can increase computational cost and risk of overfitting.

The paper claims the non-segmented approach outperforms the segmented approach by leveraging surrounding tissue information, but does not provide a detailed ablation study or statistical proof for this specific claim beyond the overall accuracy metric.

The term 'benign melanoma' is used in the text to describe benign lesions, which is clinically inaccurate terminology (melanoma is malignant); this may cause confusion regarding the nature of the benign class.

The study is a conference paper presented at the 6th International Conference on Engineering Technology and its Applications, which typically has less rigorous peer review standards compared to major journals.
